18.1: Arjuna asks for a clear explanation of the difference between renouncing action and relinquishing attachment to the results of action.
18.2: Krishna explains that renunciation means giving up actions driven by selfish desire, while true relinquishment means performing actions but letting go of attachment to their results.
18.3: Some thinkers claim all action should be abandoned because action can lead to bondage, while others teach that acts of discipline, generosity, and offering should never be abandoned.
18.4: Relinquishment itself appears in three forms depending on the qualities of Prakriti (nature) guiding a person's understanding.
18.5: Acts of generosity, discipline, and sincere offering should not be abandoned because they purify the mind and refine one's character.
18.6: Even these actions should be performed without attachment to the results, and this approach reflects the clearest form of wise action.
18.7: Abandoning one's responsibilities out of confusion or misunderstanding arises from tamas (inertia and ignorance).
18.8: Giving up responsibilities because they are uncomfortable or difficult arises from rajas (restless avoidance) and does not lead to genuine freedom.
18.9: Performing one's responsibility simply because it is right, while releasing attachment to the outcome, reflects sattva (clarity).
18.10: A person grounded in sattva (clarity) neither resists difficult work nor clings to pleasant work.
18.11: As long as one lives within a body, action cannot be fully avoided. True renunciation lies in releasing attachment to results.
18.12: Those who remain attached to the results of action experience pleasant, unpleasant, or mixed outcomes, while those who renounce attachment move beyond these results.
18.13: Every action arises through several causes rather than from a single individual agent.
18.14: The body, the sense of agency, the tools of action, the effort involved, and the surrounding conditions together produce every action.
18.15: Whatever a person does through body, speech, or mind emerges from this combination of factors.
18.16: Those who believe the individual self alone is the doer fail to see the deeper structure of action.
18.17: When ego is absent and understanding is clear, a person acts without becoming bound by the action itself.
18.18: Knowledge, the object of knowledge, and the knower initiate action, while the instrument, the action, and the agent form its structure.
18.19: Knowledge, action, and the agent appear in three forms depending on the qualities of Prakriti (nature).
18.20: Sattvic knowledge recognizes the one unchanging reality present in all beings, perceiving unity within diversity.
18.21: Rajasic knowledge sees existence as divided into many separate and competing individuals.
18.22: Tamasic knowledge clings blindly to narrow views, mistaking partial understanding for the whole truth.
18.23: Action performed as a duty, without attachment or desire for reward, reflects sattva (clarity).
18.24: Action driven by ego, ambition, and desire for results reflects rajas (restless striving).
18.25: Action performed blindly without understanding consequences reflects tamas (ignorance).
18.26: The sattvic doer acts with steadiness, enthusiasm, and freedom from ego or attachment to success or failure.
18.27: The rajasic doer is driven by craving for results and is influenced by greed, pride, and emotional swings.
18.28: The tamasic doer lacks discipline, clarity, and initiative, acting lazily and carelessly.
18.29: Understanding and determination also appear in three forms depending on the qualities of Prakriti (nature).
18.30: Sattvic understanding clearly recognizes what should be done, what should be avoided, what leads to freedom, and what leads to bondage.
18.31: Rajasic understanding confuses right and wrong and struggles to distinguish wise action from harmful action.
18.32: Tamasic understanding sees reality in a distorted way, mistaking harmful actions for righteous ones.
18.33: Sattvic determination sustains discipline of mind, senses, and life through steady inner focus.
18.34: Rajasic determination clings to achievement, pleasure, and reward through attachment and ambition.
18.35: Tamasic determination holds onto fear, laziness, grief, and destructive habits.
18.36: Happiness also appears in three forms shaped by the qualities of Prakriti (nature).
18.37: Sattvic happiness may feel difficult at first but eventually becomes deeply nourishing and peaceful.
18.38: Rajasic happiness feels pleasant at first but eventually leads to dissatisfaction and imbalance.
18.39: Tamasic happiness arises from laziness and ignorance and ultimately deepens confusion.
18.40: No being within the universe exists entirely free from the influence of the three qualities of Prakriti (nature).
18.41: Different types of responsibilities arise from the natural qualities and tendencies within individuals.
18.42: Calmness, discipline, wisdom, patience, purity, and faith arise naturally in those inclined toward contemplation and learning.
18.43: Courage, strength, leadership, and generosity arise naturally in those inclined toward protection and responsibility.
18.44: Skill in trade, agriculture, and organization arises in those inclined toward productive work, while service supports the functioning of society.
18.45: By dedicating oneself sincerely to the responsibilities aligned with one's nature, a person moves toward inner fulfillment.
18.46: Through sincere work performed in harmony with the source from which all existence arises, one moves toward realization of Brahman (the ultimate reality).
18.47: It is better to follow one's own path imperfectly than to perfectly imitate another person's path.
18.48: Every form of work contains some imperfection, just as fire is accompanied by smoke, so responsibilities should not be abandoned because of difficulty.
18.49: One who becomes inwardly detached, disciplined, and free from craving moves toward the freedom that arises through true renunciation.
18.50: Through disciplined understanding, a person who reaches this state realizes Brahman (the ultimate reality), the culmination of wisdom.
18.51: With purified understanding, self-discipline, and freedom from attraction and aversion, the mind becomes steady and clear.
18.52: Living with simplicity, moderation, reflection, and self-restraint supports the growth of deep understanding.
18.53: When ego, pride, anger, desire, and possessiveness fall away, the mind becomes peaceful and capable of realizing Brahman (the ultimate reality).
18.54: One who realizes Brahman (the ultimate reality) becomes inwardly peaceful, free from craving and sorrow, and sees all beings with equal vision.
18.55: Through deep devotion to truth, the highest reality can be fully understood, and through that understanding one becomes united with it.
18.56: Even while performing actions, one who lives in alignment with the deeper reality moves steadily toward the eternal state of freedom.
18.57: By dedicating one's actions to truth and maintaining clear understanding, the mind remains steadily aligned with the highest purpose.
18.58: When the mind remains grounded in this deeper understanding, obstacles can be overcome. But when ego dominates, confusion returns.
18.59: Even when the ego resists action, one's deeper nature continues to guide behavior.
18.60: Because each person is shaped by their own nature, they will eventually act according to that nature.
18.61: The deeper intelligence of life dwells within every heart, guiding the movement of existence as part of a vast unfolding.
18.62: By aligning oneself fully with this deeper truth, one finds lasting peace and freedom.
18.63: This wisdom has now been revealed. Reflect deeply on it and choose your path with awareness.
18.64: Krishna offers one final teaching out of deep care for Arjuna's growth and understanding.
18.65: Fix the mind on the highest truth, live in devotion to it, and dedicate actions to it. Through this alignment one moves toward the deepest realization.
18.66: Let go of rigid attachments to limited ideas of duty and take refuge in the deeper truth of existence. Through that surrender, freedom from confusion becomes possible.
18.67: This wisdom should be shared only with those who are sincere, disciplined, and open to understanding.
18.68: Those who share this teaching with sincerity and devotion help others move toward truth.
18.69: No act is more meaningful than helping others understand wisdom that leads toward freedom.
18.70: Those who study this dialogue with sincerity engage in a profound act of reflection and learning.
18.71: Even those who simply listen to this wisdom with faith and openness begin to move toward a better understanding of life.
18.72: Krishna asks Arjuna whether his confusion has been cleared through this understanding.
18.73: Arjuna responds that his confusion has disappeared, his clarity has returned, and he is ready to act with understanding.
18.74: Sanjaya describes hearing this profound dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna with great awe.
18.75: Through the insight granted by the sage Vyasa, Sanjaya was able to witness this remarkable conversation.
18.76: Remembering the dialogue between Krishna and Arjuna fills Sanjaya with deep wonder and reflection.
18.77: Remembering the vision of the universal form continues to inspire amazement and contemplation.
18.78: Where wisdom and courage unite, there arise prosperity, harmony, and success.
